case_name: Appeal of Win Win Win, LLC d/b/a/ Concord Casino & a. (New Hampshire Lottery Commission)
transcript_text:
This is case 2024-0722 and 2024-0723, appeal of win-win-win LLC DBA Concord Casino at all.

Good morning, Attorney Katz. For the record, Justice School is disqualified in this case.

Understood.

And I understand Attorney Katz, you've reserved two minutes.

That's correct.

Please proceed.

Thank you, Your Honors, and may it please the court. Adam Katz for repellence win-win-win.

Keep with the mic down a little and try and speak up.

May it please the court. Adam Katz for repellence win-win-win and Anthony Sanborn.

In late 2023, the hearings officer below held that Mr. Sanborn should have a chance to sell his business win-win-win.

Rather than accept that resolution, the state impeded and ultimately blocked a sale.

The hearings officer then found he had no power to intervene further and permitted the state to revoke win-win's license.

As Your Honors have seen by now from the record, this case comes to the court with an unusual history.

But the governing principle is familiar.

Agency action must be reasonable, lawful, and fair.

That principle, paired with the state's many waivers and concessions, points to a straightforward path to vacating the orders below and resolving this appeal as follows.

First, the state hasn't contested multiple independently dispositive issues.

For instance, the state doesn't dispute that its process here was inconsistent with the precedents at pages 46 to 50 of our opening brief.

Indeed, the state's brief does not mention any of the 12 cases we cited in that section.

Second, the state falls back on an underdeveloped standing argument.

But whether this court treats the case as an RSA 541 appeal or as a certiorari petition makes no practical difference in the end.

Either way, we have standing.

Third and finally, while the state doesn't dispute that the hearings officer erred in finding he lacked the power to prevent circumvention of the agency proceedings,

this court need not reach that issue at all.

And that's because there is no dispute that this court has wider medial authority to grant an appropriate remand.

Mr. Sandborn is not seeking to reinstate himself as a gaming operator or to re-litigate the proceedings that led to the forced sale order.

All he would like to do is sell his business and put this matter behind him.

I welcome the court's questions.

So you're essentially requesting a remand to the hearings officer.

That's correct, Your Honor.

And we've detailed exactly what we think that would look like at the conclusion of both our opening and reply briefs.

Understanding that this court could of course grant a different form of...

And that would be simply to facilitate the sale.

You're not challenging the forced sale.

That order from the hearings officer that indicated that the business was going to be sold within six months.

Correct. We're not looking to revisit the forced sale order.

I'm not asking effectively to vindicate the right we should have had in the first place to conduct the sale with a process that conformed with due process.

Do you acknowledge that the Attorney General and the Commission have broad discretion to determine suitability?

That's correct, Your Honor. We don't dispute their broad authority.

Although, again, broad authority is different from unlimited authority.

And on the substance, we think that even within a broad form of discretion,

parts of the order blocking the sale are just facially incorrect.

For instance, citing the forced sale order as a reason to prevent any sale to any buyer under any circumstance.

Under any level of discretion, I don't think that holds up.

And separately, this court has made very clear in a number of cases that on real issues,

whatever discretion is afforded to the agency, this court conducts to know of our review.

So, for example, on our due process arguments, their discretion, I don't think would factor into that at all.

On the due process issue, the substance of that issue, as I understand it,

your argument is that due process was violated because the Commission essentially refused

or declined to engage meaningfully in the review process. Is that correct?

So, I would say that that's broadly correct, Your Honor.

But I would actually disentangle it into two different things.

So, one, that's correct. They didn't meaningfully engage in the review process.

And to the point where, actually, if you look at record 14620, we sent them a chart saying,

here are sort of the core building blocks we would expect to come up in any sale agreement.

Can you provide us any guidance on this at all? And they wouldn't.

But I actually think the much more difficult question for them is the affirmative representations

that we would have opportunities to go back and forth with the state

and to cure any concerns and representations that that's how they've done it in the past.

And then, if you look at the timeline, this is sort of a dead ringer for the commerce case

where the timing of their internal determination doesn't line up at all with the actual actions they took.

So, if you look at the record starting on September, no, as early as September 9th

and certainly no later than September 27th, the agency had decided internally to block the sale

and if they continued to correspond with us for, depending on how you count,

six to eight weeks without raising any of those objections.

What are the governing principles of due process under both of those categories?

I mean, what do we look to to guide us in determining whether due process has been violated?

Sure. So, I think there are a few things in that question.

So, one, I would say this court has said that due process is a flexible standard,

particularly in administrative agency proceedings. That's number one.

Number two, what I would say is we don't think the court needs to break any new ground

in terms of fully developing what the exact contours of the process right are here

because this case so closely resembles so many of the other cases we cited in our brief

and that the commission doesn't distinguish, for instance, the Concord Steam case.

There, the agency didn't provide notice that it was going to make findings

on sort of particular aspects of the record vis-a-vis certain substantive consequences.

That's exactly what happened here.

The Portsmouth Fire Commissioner's case, I think is it maybe even a closer example

where the agency failed to follow its own announced procedures.

The appeal of Eno case where the agency gave the impression to the applicant that

they had done enough to satisfy the agency's requirements

and then pulled the rug out at the end and said, no, you haven't.

So, again, I think under any of those cases, the court could just say,

this is a close enough analog to those cases and so we're sending it back

and to try to be directly responsive to your question.

I think the through line in all of those cases, I don't think it's been formalized,

but we did our best to try to formalize it in our brief,

is agencies can't provide affirmatively misleading comments

about their own processes when it materially prejudices

the private party that's going through the agency process.

And I can hear it.

I think the prejudice is extremely pronounced for a number of reasons,

including the fact that we A, sought specific guidance on the exact terms

and the exact terms that ended up being dispositive according to the state

is one and two is if you'll recall after the state blocked the sale,

this is record two, 752 to 757.

We immediately went back and said, okay, we disagree with your rationales,

but we're willing to change everything that you said was a concern to you

and then we're still ignored and the agency wouldn't allow us to close the sale.

Let's see evidence of the internal decision to block the sale.

Sure.

So I think it's a few things.

There's, and I can pull up the exact sites so you have them,

but there were effectively a series of internal reports that led up to

as we'll call the October 30th was the final order blocking the sale.

And so you just find it.

So on September 9th, 2024, the lottery commission issued an internal report.

That's at record two, 598 to 99 where it said,

I want to be careful of the ceiling order,

but it identified two grounds to block the sale.

That was September 9th.

On September 12th, this is now record two, 503.

The lottery commission issued another report effectively summarizing

that September 9th report.

It broke no new ground of just same rationales meant blocking the sale.

On September 27th, I think, I think the agency would have a colorable argument

to say those were internal reports we hadn't made up our mind yet on blocking the sale.

Although again, I don't think that would excuse not raising noses concerns

when we made clear that we wanted to go back and forth with the agency

and when the agency had affirmatively told us that we would have a chance to do so.

September 27th at that point I think is the point of no return

where the lottery commission sends their official finding to the attorney general

saying the sale should be blocked and it provides its reasons.

And then the AG issues an internal report on October 29th

and then of course the order blocking the sale is October 30th.

So it's a series of orders and again throughout that time period

there are, just to give you one example,

which I think kind of crystallizes the point,

on September 17th, 2024 this is record two, page 633.

The state said to the buyer,

we have everything we need for a game of chance application

in terms of the finances.

You might need to provide more

if and when you submit for an HHR application

but we will let you know if that is the case.

That's September 17th, 2024.

Meanwhile, the agency had already decided to block the sale

based on that argument and then six weeks later

blocked the sale based in part on the absence of the same evidence

it said wasn't necessary and that's September 17th communication.

That's one and I think one other,

just to pilot one other that I think really crystallizes the point

October 11th.

So this is after the lottery commission has already formalized

its recommendation to block the sale.

I think there's another two, page 711.

The lottery says to the buyer,

we are still evaluating the December 28th, 2024

opening date in the lease.

But that just wasn't true.

The lottery had already said to the AG

this lease independently merits blocking the sale

in the September 27th order

and you might reasonably ask

was this just sort of a problem with the right hand

left but we know that's not true because the same signatory

to that October 11th email with the same signatory

to the report from the lottery commission saying

the sale should be blocked.

What happens on remand?

What's the jurisdiction of the hearing officer in this case?

What is his or her authority to do something?

So I think number one,

there's no dispute about this court having discretion here

so I don't want to act like I'm trying to box in the court

but what I think probably would make the most sense

to the extent this is helpful is something similar

to the appeal of behavior of sciences case where

what this court said was effectively

this process was so replete with irregularities

that the best thing to do is just send this back

to the beginning and that's really what we're asking for.

What would be the beginning?

So I think the beginning,

sorry not the beginning before sale order,

I mean if you could sort of undo time

and go back to when the hearing's officer first said

you can conduct a for sale,

that's effectively what we're asking for.

So back to December of 2023.

Correct.

One thing I do want to clarify,

we've asked for 12 months instead of 9,

this court could decide 6 is appropriate,

it could decide 15 is appropriate.

The reason we asked for 12 instead of 9 is just a very,

there's no magic to it, it's just that

I can represent that all of the buyers,

this is a surprisingly tight knit community of buyers,

their impression is this is a hostile regulator

and that's a position we were not in

in December 2023 and so having the extra time

to be able to effectively assuage buyers

that this regulator is not going to

retaliate against you effectively

is the basis we're asking for 12 instead of 9 months.

Do you concede that the petitioner

doesn't have standing on the suitability issue?

There's no standing?

No, do we concede that there's no standing?

No, no, no, we strongly oppose that point

I think for a few reasons.

Well, assuming we find there is no standing,

do you concede that there's a different standard

of review that applies to certiorary?

Oh I see, you're saying do I concede

that there's no standing under 541?

So if you were to find that 541 didn't apply,

what's your position on that issue?

Great, so a few things, one I would say

in the petition of Donovan case what this court said

was the substantive standard for certiorary

versus 541 is not altogether dissimilar

actually in both cases what you're really asking

is did the agency act unreasonably or unlawfully

and I think you can actually see it operationalized

in the petition of Donovan case

because there were some appeals from agencies

in 541 and other appeals that were subject to certiorary

and the court just analyzed them all collectively

so that's sort of the framing point

and the state hasn't made the argument that

on sort of X standard of deference

they would lose but under X plus 5 they would win

so I actually don't think that on the substantive side

it makes a difference.

In terms of standing, their argument is based

on the Silver Brothers case

and I think once you get past the sort of superficial

similarity of the Silver Brothers case

it just is miles apart from this case.

So number one, in the Silver Brothers case

the sale was only hypothetical.

This court pointed out that no one had even applied

for the sale so that's number one.

Number two, in that case they were challenging

a durational residency requirement

which only operated upon the buyer and not the seller

and again that's the total opposite of this case where

I can read out some quotes to the court

but the suitability order is very clear

that it's making a finding both with respect to the buyer

and with respect to win-win-win

which is an appellant in this case

and not only that it's actually more harmful

to win-win because the buyer could go out

and buy a different entity tomorrow

the order is quite explicit that win-win-win

under the view of the state

is unlikely to be able to sell to anyone

under any circumstance at any time

irrespective of the buyer.

So that's number two on the Silver Brothers case.

Number three is there's an important difference

in the two statutory regimes at issue

so in Silver Brothers it was a liquor license

and in the liquor licensing scheme

you can apply for a license

and then separately go out and buy an entity.

Number 287D4

you can't transfer a license

and the entity comes with certain privileges

and so the only way to operationalize a sale

is to buy the entity itself

and so necessarily what you're doing

is presenting a transaction to the state

and again we've set this out in our brief

but it's quite clear that what the state was doing

was not just evaluating a buyer

or a seller but a transaction involving them both

I think that the AG's internal report

from October 29th

refers to a proposed transaction

two times in five pages

so that's number three and then number four I think

even if none of that were true

in this case unlike in the Silver Brothers case

Mr. Sam Warren and Win Win Win

are the recipients of a fore sale order

that said

the hearings officer was saying the way I want to resolve this

is you have a right to conduct a fore sale

and so to the extent that's impeded upon

that's a freestanding basis for standing

so again I don't think the Silver Brothers case

works for them at all.

Thank you very much. Turn catch your red light sign.

just to make one last point

I think one sort of inescapable part of the state's position

in this case which I think your colloquium

that with attorney trick is if they are correct

on standing it would mean that

we could have received an order saying you can conduct

a foresale to a state approved buyer

the state could withhold that approval for unlawful

reasons they could say

we're not especially fond of Andy Sandborn or something

to that effect and we would have no recourse and that

just can't be right.

Thank you very much council

case submitted

segments.text: This is case 2024-0722 and 2024-0723, appeal of win-win-win LLC DBA Concord Casino at all.
Good morning, Attorney Katz. For the record, Justice School is disqualified in this case.
Understood.
And I understand Attorney Katz, you've reserved two minutes.
That's correct.
Please proceed.
Thank you, Your Honors, and may it please the court. Adam Katz for repellence win-win-win.
Keep with the mic down a little and try and speak up.
May it please the court. Adam Katz for repellence win-win-win and Anthony Sanborn.
In late 2023, the hearings officer below held that Mr. Sanborn should have a chance to sell his business win-win-win.
Rather than accept that resolution, the state impeded and ultimately blocked a sale.
The hearings officer then found he had no power to intervene further and permitted the state to revoke win-win's license.
As Your Honors have seen by now from the record, this case comes to the court with an unusual history.
But the governing principle is familiar.
Agency action must be reasonable, lawful, and fair.
That principle, paired with the state's many waivers and concessions, points to a straightforward path to vacating the orders below and resolving this appeal as follows.
First, the state hasn't contested multiple independently dispositive issues.
For instance, the state doesn't dispute that its process here was inconsistent with the precedents at pages 46 to 50 of our opening brief.
Indeed, the state's brief does not mention any of the 12 cases we cited in that section.
Second, the state falls back on an underdeveloped standing argument.
But whether this court treats the case as an RSA 541 appeal or as a certiorari petition makes no practical difference in the end.
Either way, we have standing.
Third and finally, while the state doesn't dispute that the hearings officer erred in finding he lacked the power to prevent circumvention of the agency proceedings,
this court need not reach that issue at all.
And that's because there is no dispute that this court has wider medial authority to grant an appropriate remand.
Mr. Sandborn is not seeking to reinstate himself as a gaming operator or to re-litigate the proceedings that led to the forced sale order.
All he would like to do is sell his business and put this matter behind him.
I welcome the court's questions.
So you're essentially requesting a remand to the hearings officer.
That's correct, Your Honor.
And we've detailed exactly what we think that would look like at the conclusion of both our opening and reply briefs.
Understanding that this court could of course grant a different form of...
And that would be simply to facilitate the sale.
You're not challenging the forced sale.
That order from the hearings officer that indicated that the business was going to be sold within six months.
Correct. We're not looking to revisit the forced sale order.
I'm not asking effectively to vindicate the right we should have had in the first place to conduct the sale with a process that conformed with due process.
Do you acknowledge that the Attorney General and the Commission have broad discretion to determine suitability?
That's correct, Your Honor. We don't dispute their broad authority.
Although, again, broad authority is different from unlimited authority.
And on the substance, we think that even within a broad form of discretion,
parts of the order blocking the sale are just facially incorrect.
For instance, citing the forced sale order as a reason to prevent any sale to any buyer under any circumstance.
Under any level of discretion, I don't think that holds up.
And separately, this court has made very clear in a number of cases that on real issues,
whatever discretion is afforded to the agency, this court conducts to know of our review.
So, for example, on our due process arguments, their discretion, I don't think would factor into that at all.
On the due process issue, the substance of that issue, as I understand it,
your argument is that due process was violated because the Commission essentially refused
or declined to engage meaningfully in the review process. Is that correct?
So, I would say that that's broadly correct, Your Honor.
But I would actually disentangle it into two different things.
So, one, that's correct. They didn't meaningfully engage in the review process.
And to the point where, actually, if you look at record 14620, we sent them a chart saying,
here are sort of the core building blocks we would expect to come up in any sale agreement.
Can you provide us any guidance on this at all? And they wouldn't.
But I actually think the much more difficult question for them is the affirmative representations
that we would have opportunities to go back and forth with the state
and to cure any concerns and representations that that's how they've done it in the past.
And then, if you look at the timeline, this is sort of a dead ringer for the commerce case
where the timing of their internal determination doesn't line up at all with the actual actions they took.
So, if you look at the record starting on September, no, as early as September 9th
and certainly no later than September 27th, the agency had decided internally to block the sale
and if they continued to correspond with us for, depending on how you count,
six to eight weeks without raising any of those objections.
What are the governing principles of due process under both of those categories?
I mean, what do we look to to guide us in determining whether due process has been violated?
Sure. So, I think there are a few things in that question.
So, one, I would say this court has said that due process is a flexible standard,
particularly in administrative agency proceedings. That's number one.
Number two, what I would say is we don't think the court needs to break any new ground
in terms of fully developing what the exact contours of the process right are here
because this case so closely resembles so many of the other cases we cited in our brief
and that the commission doesn't distinguish, for instance, the Concord Steam case.
There, the agency didn't provide notice that it was going to make findings
on sort of particular aspects of the record vis-a-vis certain substantive consequences.
That's exactly what happened here.
The Portsmouth Fire Commissioner's case, I think is it maybe even a closer example
where the agency failed to follow its own announced procedures.
The appeal of Eno case where the agency gave the impression to the applicant that
they had done enough to satisfy the agency's requirements
and then pulled the rug out at the end and said, no, you haven't.
So, again, I think under any of those cases, the court could just say,
this is a close enough analog to those cases and so we're sending it back
and to try to be directly responsive to your question.
I think the through line in all of those cases, I don't think it's been formalized,
but we did our best to try to formalize it in our brief,
is agencies can't provide affirmatively misleading comments
about their own processes when it materially prejudices
the private party that's going through the agency process.
And I can hear it.
I think the prejudice is extremely pronounced for a number of reasons,
including the fact that we A, sought specific guidance on the exact terms
and the exact terms that ended up being dispositive according to the state
is one and two is if you'll recall after the state blocked the sale,
this is record two, 752 to 757.
We immediately went back and said, okay, we disagree with your rationales,
but we're willing to change everything that you said was a concern to you
and then we're still ignored and the agency wouldn't allow us to close the sale.
Let's see evidence of the internal decision to block the sale.
Sure.
So I think it's a few things.
There's, and I can pull up the exact sites so you have them,
but there were effectively a series of internal reports that led up to
as we'll call the October 30th was the final order blocking the sale.
And so you just find it.
So on September 9th, 2024, the lottery commission issued an internal report.
That's at record two, 598 to 99 where it said,
I want to be careful of the ceiling order,
but it identified two grounds to block the sale.
That was September 9th.
On September 12th, this is now record two, 503.
The lottery commission issued another report effectively summarizing
that September 9th report.
It broke no new ground of just same rationales meant blocking the sale.
On September 27th, I think, I think the agency would have a colorable argument
to say those were internal reports we hadn't made up our mind yet on blocking the sale.
Although again, I don't think that would excuse not raising noses concerns
when we made clear that we wanted to go back and forth with the agency
and when the agency had affirmatively told us that we would have a chance to do so.
September 27th at that point I think is the point of no return
where the lottery commission sends their official finding to the attorney general
saying the sale should be blocked and it provides its reasons.
And then the AG issues an internal report on October 29th
and then of course the order blocking the sale is October 30th.
So it's a series of orders and again throughout that time period
there are, just to give you one example,
which I think kind of crystallizes the point,
on September 17th, 2024 this is record two, page 633.
The state said to the buyer,
we have everything we need for a game of chance application
in terms of the finances.
You might need to provide more
if and when you submit for an HHR application
but we will let you know if that is the case.
That's September 17th, 2024.
Meanwhile, the agency had already decided to block the sale
based on that argument and then six weeks later
blocked the sale based in part on the absence of the same evidence
it said wasn't necessary and that's September 17th communication.
That's one and I think one other,
just to pilot one other that I think really crystallizes the point
October 11th.
So this is after the lottery commission has already formalized
its recommendation to block the sale.
I think there's another two, page 711.
The lottery says to the buyer,
we are still evaluating the December 28th, 2024
opening date in the lease.
But that just wasn't true.
The lottery had already said to the AG
this lease independently merits blocking the sale
in the September 27th order
and you might reasonably ask
was this just sort of a problem with the right hand
left but we know that's not true because the same signatory
to that October 11th email with the same signatory
to the report from the lottery commission saying
the sale should be blocked.
What happens on remand?
What's the jurisdiction of the hearing officer in this case?
What is his or her authority to do something?
So I think number one,
there's no dispute about this court having discretion here
so I don't want to act like I'm trying to box in the court
but what I think probably would make the most sense
to the extent this is helpful is something similar
to the appeal of behavior of sciences case where
what this court said was effectively
this process was so replete with irregularities
that the best thing to do is just send this back
to the beginning and that's really what we're asking for.
What would be the beginning?
So I think the beginning,
sorry not the beginning before sale order,
I mean if you could sort of undo time
and go back to when the hearing's officer first said
you can conduct a for sale,
that's effectively what we're asking for.
So back to December of 2023.
Correct.
One thing I do want to clarify,
we've asked for 12 months instead of 9,
this court could decide 6 is appropriate,
it could decide 15 is appropriate.
The reason we asked for 12 instead of 9 is just a very,
there's no magic to it, it's just that
I can represent that all of the buyers,
this is a surprisingly tight knit community of buyers,
their impression is this is a hostile regulator
and that's a position we were not in
in December 2023 and so having the extra time
to be able to effectively assuage buyers
that this regulator is not going to
retaliate against you effectively
is the basis we're asking for 12 instead of 9 months.
Do you concede that the petitioner
doesn't have standing on the suitability issue?
There's no standing?
No, do we concede that there's no standing?
No, no, no, we strongly oppose that point
I think for a few reasons.
Well, assuming we find there is no standing,
do you concede that there's a different standard
of review that applies to certiorary?
Oh I see, you're saying do I concede
that there's no standing under 541?
So if you were to find that 541 didn't apply,
what's your position on that issue?
Great, so a few things, one I would say
in the petition of Donovan case what this court said
was the substantive standard for certiorary
versus 541 is not altogether dissimilar
actually in both cases what you're really asking
is did the agency act unreasonably or unlawfully
and I think you can actually see it operationalized
in the petition of Donovan case
because there were some appeals from agencies
in 541 and other appeals that were subject to certiorary
and the court just analyzed them all collectively
so that's sort of the framing point
and the state hasn't made the argument that
on sort of X standard of deference
they would lose but under X plus 5 they would win
so I actually don't think that on the substantive side
it makes a difference.
In terms of standing, their argument is based
on the Silver Brothers case
and I think once you get past the sort of superficial
similarity of the Silver Brothers case
it just is miles apart from this case.
So number one, in the Silver Brothers case
the sale was only hypothetical.
This court pointed out that no one had even applied
for the sale so that's number one.
Number two, in that case they were challenging
a durational residency requirement
which only operated upon the buyer and not the seller
and again that's the total opposite of this case where
I can read out some quotes to the court
but the suitability order is very clear
that it's making a finding both with respect to the buyer
and with respect to win-win-win
which is an appellant in this case
and not only that it's actually more harmful
to win-win because the buyer could go out
and buy a different entity tomorrow
the order is quite explicit that win-win-win
under the view of the state
is unlikely to be able to sell to anyone
under any circumstance at any time
irrespective of the buyer.
So that's number two on the Silver Brothers case.
Number three is there's an important difference
in the two statutory regimes at issue
so in Silver Brothers it was a liquor license
and in the liquor licensing scheme
you can apply for a license
and then separately go out and buy an entity.
Number 287D4
you can't transfer a license
and the entity comes with certain privileges
and so the only way to operationalize a sale
is to buy the entity itself
and so necessarily what you're doing
is presenting a transaction to the state
and again we've set this out in our brief
but it's quite clear that what the state was doing
was not just evaluating a buyer
or a seller but a transaction involving them both
I think that the AG's internal report
from October 29th
refers to a proposed transaction
two times in five pages
so that's number three and then number four I think
even if none of that were true
in this case unlike in the Silver Brothers case
Mr. Sam Warren and Win Win Win
are the recipients of a fore sale order
that said
the hearings officer was saying the way I want to resolve this
is you have a right to conduct a fore sale
and so to the extent that's impeded upon
that's a freestanding basis for standing
so again I don't think the Silver Brothers case
works for them at all.
Thank you very much. Turn catch your red light sign.

Very briefly
so a few things just to pick up on attorney tricks
presentation.
First, under
attorney trick mentioned we could have appealed the December 27th
23 order.
Attorney tricks said we could have appealed the December 27th
23 order.
There's no way we could have appealed that order

so I just want to make that point clear.
The state said

That's certainly not the experience of the buyers

you will see that the state said that the AG

The state mentioned

this court found that there still was a live controversy

On the HHR point
again we think it is just a violation of foundational
agency principles to raise a brand new argument on appeal
but I would point the court to a few other things

One at record 2281 the agency told

for HHR so this really was
an about face in this court
and also even if the agency were correct
that Win-Win-Win were not eligible for HHR
the whole thrust of their argument is that that would
in perpetuity
tether Mr. Sandborn to
the entity to the buyer but that's actually just not true
if you look at section 2.4C
of the merger agreement you will see
and I want to be careful of the ceiling agreement but
